item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow nobody has commented in 5 years O_o I just have to say I loved the part with 076-2 though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a colloquial way to say "I'm not very enthusiastic about it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"it ain't blowin' my skirt up."
What does this mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You get a very solid no-vote from me. Excellent idea, with over-the-top violence. I'd much rather see this without the "unstoppable killing machine" aspect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if you kill 1669 after 1669-x spawns but before the latter gets a chance to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has someone been watching Jet Li movies recently?
S'okay, this was well-written and intriguing enough that any similarity was not a detriment to enjoyment.

There are one or two bits of less than perfectly formal tone, "shrug off" injury for example. But they don't really detract from enjoyment either.

Well done, and I'd like to know more about what's going on behind the scenes that motivates all these dopplegangers. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love Foundation staff SCPs with off the wall and inexplicable effects. This one pulls it off very nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, that's delightfully bizarre. I love that sort of article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I agree, its just my curiosity is itching.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1319. It's really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which one is that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has the foundation considered what would happen if they were to kill the current instance before the manifestatuion of the copy?

I'd say they have considered that. What you need to take into account though, is that the copy is not randomly killing the original. The description specifically mentions "it will attempt to limit collateral damage — especially injury or loss of life"; the copy is displaying not just an understanding of what it's doing, but an understanding with a benevolent inclination towards the Foundation.
The copy has reasons for its actions, but is for some unknown reason unable or unwilling to explain them directly, either to the original or the Foundation.

Killing the current manifestation might interrupt whatever is going on here. And given the "friendly" disposition of the copy (unless that itself is a ruse to build up a clone army of frozen maimed corpses), whatever is going on, is likely not detrimental to the Foundation.

Interrupting the current chain of events, which so far remains containable, is an experiment with little to gain, since it likely wouldn't answer any questions, and an uncertain amount to lose, where, given the nature of the anomaly, "uncertain" can quickly escalate into very bad things.

Right now, the Foundation can afford to wait. And I figure that's exactly what they're doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCPs closely connected to the Foundation tend to be really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing the second option, but with some differences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost as cool as the Researcher who split in half. I really like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice idea but I do have one question I would like to ask. Has the foundation considered what would happen if they were to kill the current instance before the manifestatuion of the copy? I can understand the reluctance of the foundation to risk permenent destruction of the SCP but I just wondered if the copy would pre-empt the foundation and get there first or just rock up 161-X days later wondering where the missed days have gone.

e: of course the third and less interesting option is he is just dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea, good execution, funny title, and as usual the Foundation SCPs are really interesting and amusing. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty neat idea. Grabs me just enough to upvote, if there were any more of the log I'd probably either upvote more enthusiastically or downvote with extreme prejudice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A neat little idea, but there's not enough in the article to hold my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Proper procedure, internal consistency whilst outwardly seemingly inexplicable, implied Euclid threat. Nice!

It might be worthwhile to showcase the Foundation's pragmatism in the containment procedure by requiring the unsuspecting SCP-1669 to be in a controlled space containing "suitable equipment" somewhere around 161 days after an event.
That way incidental collateral damage might be avoided and, in a somewhat morbid way, his inevitable suffering would be minimized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It's a solid piece, but it ain't blowin' my skirt up. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty enjoyable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hehehe.

I was sort of expecting subtle deviations from the baseline to start showing up regularly (not mentioned in the Description, of course, to keep the surprise), but this still works fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea and writing is very good. Let down, I think, by having the new instances being cartoonish action heroes. With something bettter thought out to explain how the new instances kill the old everytime I would happily upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I groaned when I understood the pun in the title.

Anyways, this is not poorly written, but I'm not really drawn in by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So someone replacing himself with copies of himself periodically.
I like it.
